title: Directing
summary: Directing is leading, influencing, and motivating employees to perform assigned tasks. It is the heart of management — where plans are executed.
4 elements of Directing: Supervision, Motivation, Leadership, Communication.
Motivation: The process of stimulating people to act to accomplish desired goals. Maslow's Hierarchy: Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-actualisation.
Incentives: Financial (Pay, Bonus, Profit-sharing, Stock options) and Non-financial (Status, Recognition, Job security, Career growth, Participation).
Leadership styles: Autocratic (leader decides alone), Democratic (leader consults team), Laissez-faire (leader gives full freedom).
Communication: The process of exchanging information between sender and receiver. Formal (official channels) and Informal (grapevine).
Barriers to communication: Semantic, Psychological, Organisational, Personal. Overcoming: Clarify ideas, use simple language, listen actively, encourage feedback.

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: If the question says 'full freedom to subordinates' → Laissez-faire. 'Leader consults before deciding' → Democratic. 'Leader decides alone' → Autocratic. For Maslow: 'Basic salary' → Physiological. 'Pension/job security' → Safety. 'Friendly colleagues' → Social. 'Job title/recognition' → Esteem. 'Challenging work/achievement' → Self-actualisation.

sections.title: Meaning of Directing

definition.text: The process of instructing, guiding, counselling, motivating, and leading people in the organisation to achieve its objectives.

sections.content: Directing initiates action — it is the function that gets work done through people. It operates at every level of management.

sections.title: Elements of Directing
points: 1. Supervision: Overseeing work of subordinates to ensure tasks are done as planned.
2. Motivation: Stimulating people to act willingly towards organisational goals.
3. Leadership: Influencing others to work willingly toward group objectives.
4. Communication: Transfer of information, ideas, and understanding from sender to receiver.

sections.title: Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs
points: 1. Physiological Needs (Basic): Food, shelter, clothing. In organisation: Basic salary.
2. Safety/Security Needs: Protection from danger, stability. In organisation: Job security, pension plan.
3. Social/Belongingness Needs: Friendship, acceptance. In organisation: Cordial relations with colleagues.
4. Esteem Needs: Status, recognition, respect. In organisation: Job title, promotions.
5. Self-Actualisation Needs (Highest): Realising full potential, personal growth. In organisation: Challenging work, achievement of goals.
Key point: Needs follow a hierarchy — a satisfied need no longer motivates. Once physiological needs are met, safety needs become the motivator, and so on.

sections.title: Financial & Non-Financial Incentives
points: Financial Incentives: Pay & allowances, Productivity-linked wages, Bonus, Profit-sharing, Co-partnership/Stock options, Retirement benefits, Perquisites (car, housing, medical).
Non-Financial Incentives: Status, Career advancement, Job enrichment, Job security, Employee recognition, Employee participation, Autonomy/Empowerment.

sections.title: Leadership Styles
points: 1. Autocratic/Authoritarian: Leader takes all decisions without consulting subordinates. Centralised authority. Quick decisions but low morale.
2. Democratic/Participative: Leader consults subordinates before making decisions. Shared authority. Higher morale and commitment.
3. Laissez-faire/Free Rein: Leader gives full freedom to subordinates to make decisions. Minimal supervision. Works only with highly skilled, self-motivated teams.

sections.title: Communication
sections.content: Communication is the process of exchange of information between a sender and receiver through a chosen channel/medium.
points: Elements: Sender → Encoding → Message → Channel → Decoding → Receiver → Feedback. Noise can interfere at any stage.
Formal Communication: Follows official chain of command. Types: Downward (orders), Upward (reports), Horizontal (same level).
Informal Communication (Grapevine): Arises from social interactions. Faster but may carry rumours. Cannot be eliminated — managers should use it wisely.

sections.title: Barriers to Communication
points: 1. Semantic Barriers: Words interpreted differently, technical jargon, body language mismatch.
2. Psychological Barriers: Premature evaluation, lack of attention, distrust, emotions.
3. Organisational Barriers: Rigid policies, too many levels, complex structure.
4. Personal Barriers: Fear, lack of confidence, unwillingness to communicate.
Overcoming: Clarify ideas before communicating, use simple language, be a good listener, encourage feedback, ensure proper medium, be aware of body language.

tables.title: Leadership Styles Comparison
tables.tip: Very common 4-mark question. Know all three styles clearly.

questions.text: A leadership style where subordinates are given full freedom is called:
Explain Maslow's hierarchy with respect to organisational needs for any three levels.
questions.answer:
(i) Physiological: Basic salary to meet food, shelter, clothing needs.
(ii) Safety: Job security, pension plans to provide stability and protection.
(iii) Esteem: Job titles, recognition, promotions to satisfy the need for status and respect.

questions.text: State any three barriers to effective communication.
questions.answer:
(i) Semantic: Same words interpreted differently or use of technical jargon.
(ii) Psychological: Premature evaluation, distrust, or emotional state of receiver.
(iii) Organisational: Rigid policies, too many hierarchical levels distorting the message.

questions.text:
A manager at XYZ Ltd motivates employees by recognising their achievements publicly and involving them in decision-making. He also gives team leaders freedom to plan and execute their projects independently.

Identify the elements of directing and leadership style.
questions.answer:
1. Motivation: 'Recognising achievements publicly' — non-financial incentive (employee recognition).
2. Leadership (Democratic): 'Involving them in decision-making' — consulting subordinates before decisions.
3. Leadership (Laissez-faire): 'Giving freedom to plan and execute independently' — full autonomy to capable team leaders.

pyqs.text: Explain Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs with reference to organisational context.
pyqs.answer: Maslow identified 5 levels: (1) Physiological — basic salary. (2) Safety — job security, pension. (3) Social — cordial relations with colleagues. (4) Esteem — job title, recognition. (5) Self-actualisation — challenging work, personal growth. A satisfied need no longer motivates — managers must identify which level employees are at and offer the appropriate incentive.

pyqs.text: Distinguish between formal and informal communication.
pyqs.answer:
(1) Formal follows official channels; informal arises from social interactions.
(2) Formal is slower but reliable; informal is fast but may carry rumours.
(3) Formal can be controlled by management; informal cannot.

related_chapters.title: Principles of Management
related_chapters.reason: Taylor's Mental Revolution relates to motivation

related_chapters.title: Staffing
related_chapters.reason: After placing employees, they need direction
